web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/VU-NiioUpxg/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [iOS Agent Skills, App Store Connect CLI, Foundation Models Tokens & More](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VU-NiioUpxg)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Sean Allen](https://www.youtube.com/@seanallen)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-03-26
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 13:49
**Views**: 12251
**Likes**: 441
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
Check out WinWinKit - https://winwinkit.com
🔗 Swift News Link Repo - https://github.com/SAllen0400/swift-news
My Swift & SwiftUI Courses - https://seanallen.teachable.com/
Swift News Newsletter - https://swiftnews.kit.com/newsletter

In this episode of Swift News I keep you up to date on the latest in greatest in the world of iOS development, Swift and SwiftUI. I discuss the following topics:

- App Store Connect CLI
- iOS Agent Skills
- Swift Concurrency Learning Roadmap
- Foundation Models Token Tracking
- AI Workflow Context Switching

Eraser: Cleaner for Xcode -
https://apps.apple.com/us/app/eraser-cleaner-for-xcode/id6758531046

https://seanallen.teachable.com/p/call

Sean Allen - https://twitter.com/seanallen_dev

https://apps.apple.com/app/formula-widgets-push-push/id6744127751

https://apps.apple.com/app/roast-app-roast-your-friends/id6743675883

https://appscreens.com/?via=8d5841

https://www.bigmountainstudio.com/a/fzc51

Antoine Van Der Lee - Going Indie Course:
https://courses.avanderlee.com/p/from-side-project-to-going-indie?affcode=2082304_o9res6rn

Paul Hudson's Hacking With Swift:
https://gumroad.com/a/762098803

RocketSim - Enhance Your Xcode Simulator:
https://gumroad.com/a/51797971/ftvbh

Timestamps:
0:00 - App Store Connect CLI
2:28 - WinWinKit
3:57 - iOS Agent Skills
6:31 - Swift Concurrency Learning Roadmap
8:46 - Foundation Models Token Tracking
10:56 - AI Workflow Context Switching

#swift #swiftui #iosdevelopment
```

## Transcript

First up, I want to share the App Store Connect CLI or command line interface by RDR rank rim. And
essentially what this is is anything you can do in App Store Connect using the App Store Connect API,
you can now do in the terminal or a command line interface. Now, you may not be a CLI person. I'm
not really a CLI person. So, I'm like, eh, what's the big deal? Well, I'll tell you why. Well,
aside from all the things you can do, right? You can automate everything with apps connecting the
full app store connect API. That's test flight distribution, app store submission, screenshots,
localization of your metadata. Cool. You can type these command line commands and do all that. Now,
I'm not dismissing that. I know a lot of people love the command line. That's just not for me and
it may not be for you. But why I'm excited about this and want to share it is because now you have
agent skills where now Codeex Cloud Code can do all this stuff for you via the App Store Connect
CLI. And there's a whole number of them. You can build and archive your projects. There's a whole
end toend release flow. Submit your builds to test flight. There's metadata localization. You know,
if your app has six different languages, you know, localizing that metadata for every single language
on every single release, right? Every what's new in this version, you got to do for every language.
And what I was doing is I was sending it to Chat GBT, hey, translate this. And then I was copying
and pasting into each individual location. And even though chatbt was doing the translation for
me, that was still very tedious and timeconuming. Well, now the agent can just localize all that
metadata for you. And then another huge one that was timeconuming, PPP pricing or purchasing power
parody pricing. This is where you change your app's pricing based on location. For example,
the price in the United States may be more expensive than the price in a country where their
currency isn't doing as well. And if you did this in App Store Connect, you'd have to go into every
single country, change the price for every single country. It is uh it's rough. But again, now the
agents can just do all that for you in the click of a button. And it's been fun watching Road Rank
build this in public. If you follow him, you know he uh tweets about it a lot. Sorry, Roadrank, but
you do tweet about it a lot. But it's been cool to watch it happen. And I haven't actually tried this
yet, full disclosure, because I was waiting for it to mature a little bit cuz he started
tweeting about this like from the beginning, but it's now finally to a place and this is also I
want to share. It's like, all right, now it's time to dive in, give my agent some App Store Connect
superpower and and give this a shot. And if you're curious, you can check out the GitHub repo. As you
can see, it has 3.3K stars. It's taken off. It says over 10,000 downloads, I believe. But yeah,
you can dive in to the repo, see what the code is doing. It is open source. And then of course
there's a readme with, you know, troubleshooting, getting started, support, references, all kinds of
stuff there. So check out the App Store Connect CLI. Let me know what you think about it.
Today's episode of Swift News is sponsored by Win-win Kit. And this allows you to create an
affiliate program for your app. So whether you're working with influencers or a newsletter to market
your app or your app has a large community of users, now when they refer someone new to your
app using the offer code and they purchase your app, they can receive a commission. And using
affiliates is a classic growth and marketing channel that you can now explore with your app.
And you can customize that commission like per affiliate that they receive,
whether you want it to be 25%, 30%. And you can customize the duration. Do they only get
commission on the first renewal? Like say it's a monthly app, they only get it on the first month
or you could let them have it for the first 6 months to like really entice them. The point is
you can customize the commission percentage and the duration however you like. Now, of course,
Win-win kit can be implemented via an SDK. But a really nice bonus here is that there's a no
code option. So, if you're a larger app that has a marketing team, or even if you're smaller and you
just have someone helping you out on a grow side and they're not technical, they don't have to mess
with the SDK or any code at all, they can handle this affiliate program all on their own because
it integrates with App Store Connect using an API key and all you need is the sales and marketing
permissions. And now your sales or marketing team can create affiliate codes right in the
Windwin Kit dashboard and everything happens in the background. No code necessary. And as you can
see, you can track all your affiliates, see how they're doing, see what kind of revenue they're
generating, see how much you've paid them out. You can group them. You see influencers, community,
media. So, you can really track how this affiliate program is doing in your app. So, check out WinWin
Kit if you think an affiliate program will work for your app. The link is in the description.
Now, let's talk about even more skills that you can give your AI agents to help you build
the best iOS apps. And Paul Hudson has released a few different skills. As you can see, there's
the Swift UI agent skills. This helps you write really good Swift UI. We're going to dig into
this in a second, but I want to show that he's also built one for Swift concurrency. You know,
there's a lot of gotchas with Swift concurrency, especially when the AI agent is writing it. So,
this will help steer the agent. Swift data, same case, and their Swift testing. And then Paul's got
a whole repo of all kinds of Swift agent skills that you can have. And of course, in typical Paul
fashion, he shows you how to use it too via an article. So, let's dive into the Swift UI skill
just to give you an example of what kind of stuff this can do. And the reason this is important
because if you've used any AI agents trying to write Swift UI, you know the LLM's like training
data has a certain cut off date. Maybe that was 6 months ago, maybe it was a year ago, right? That's
always a moving target obviously, but SwiftUI is constantly changing and all the stuff the LLM was
trained on could have been older SwiftUI blog posts, older Swift UI videos. So what ends up
happening is you get some deprecated stuff in your Swift UI or just, you know, Swift UI has changed a
lot to it's old Swift UI. Well, that's what Paul's agent skill does is basically course corrects the
LLM to write the most recent best practice Swift UI. So, let's click into Swift UI Pro just to
show you an example of that here. So, we'll go to references and then this is just the API one. So,
again, just some examples, right? Always use foreground style instead of foreground color.
Foreground color was deprecated, I believe, iOS 15. Could be wrong on that, but a couple years
ago, but sometimes you'll have the LLM still using these old modifiers. Same thing with clip shape.
rather than corner radius. And you can see a whole ton of examples of Paul's skill steering the LLM
to the most modern best practice Swift UI. Here's another example. This concatenated text where you
add the plus in between two text views that is now deprecated. So when you use this skill,
it's going to use the more proper modern way to not do that anymore. So again, the whole point
of these skills, and that was just Swift UI, he's got one of Swift concurrency, Swift data,
is to correct the LLM. basically give the LLM a PhD in modern Swift, Swift concurrency,
Swift UI to help you write better code or help you review better code that the AI writes, save you
time either way. And it's not just Paul uh Thomas Ricard who has recently been hired at OpenAI
on the Codeex team. So, congrats to him. He also has uh some cool skills to use. There's a whole
Swift UI liquid glass skill give you the best practices converting your app to liquid glass.
Swift UI performance audit. You may be aware, you may not be aware. You can write Swift UI in a way
that's not very performant. you get hitchy scrolling and there is a way to write very
performant Swift UI code and that's what this agent will do. It will go through and audit
that for you. So yeah, if you're using Cloud Code or Codeex to help you build your apps and
it's writing Swift UI, Swift concurrency, all that stuff, these skills are are just a must.
All right, enough of the AI agent stuff for now. It's just it's hard not to talk about like our
whole job is changing. The whole way we do things is changing. So I know you may be getting sick of
hearing about AI agents and skills and all this stuff. So we we'll get back to some regular old
Swift stuff, but here's Swift concurrency. Let's say you don't want to rely on AI just writing all
the code and you actually want to know your [ __ ] Uh which I highly recommend you do anyway because
yeah, you don't want to just 100% blindly rely on the AI. Quick little side tangent though because
I often think about how difficult it would be to learn right now because it is very tempting
just to let the AI do it. Like I and many others are very fortunate to have uh learned the hard
way so to speak over the past decade. So you have that base knowledge to help you use the AI. But I
do try to put myself in the newbie's position is like, man, it'd be really hard to try to
like learn all this stuff when the ease of AI is like right there. But I do recommend you do learn
it. And that's where this post comes in. Swift concurrency from zero to hero, a reading list.
So what Alex has done here is basically compiled a short little road map. Now there's tons of other
resources out there. He doesn't claim for this to be a comprehensive list, but if you're getting
started with Swift Concurrency, you're like, "Ah, I really need to dive into that." or even
if you've learned it and back to those AI agents like I feel like my skills have atrophied because
I have been relying on AI agents. I haven't been having to like bang my head against the wall,
learn things and like do it myself. So whether you're a newbie or you feel like your skills have
atrophied, which I put myself in that bucket, this is a great road map to revisit to just drill the
fundamentals, review the basics. But level zero effing approachable swift concurrency. It's an
easy and fun read. Lots of playful analogies. It's a great introduction to the topic if you've never
even like dealt with or seen swift concurrency. you know, level one, the there's a step-by-step
series by Matt Masakott. This was pivotal in how I learned Swift Concurrency initially. So,
I highly recommend Matt's content, but it takes you into there and then, you know, level two,
level three. Once you get into the higher levels, you know, you're actually reading the uh Swift
evolution proposals. That really gets into the weeds. And Alex brings up a good point is that
just knowing what and how isn't enough. You also need to understand why behind like the
currency features even exist. That really helps you understand like why does this even need to
be sendable? Like, what's the whole idea behind something being sendable? like why is that a big
deal and that really helps you understand how swift concurrency works. So anyway just yeah a
great little road map. I thought this was nice because swift concurrency is a very daunting
topic. So a nice road map to start at level zero up to more advanced is a great tool to have.
Next up we got a nice little addition to foundation models in iOS 26.4 and that is
tracking token usage in foundation models. So if you're not aware foundation models is the ondevice
LLM that Apple uses. However, the ondevice model has a context window of 4,96 tokens,
which is insanely tiny. That is so small. And like they say, so understanding how many tokens your
instructions, prompts, and tools consume, that's essential for building reliable features. Because
it is so small, you have to be very intelligent, very efficient, and very careful with how you
use those tokens. Those tokens are very precious because there's so many of them. And basically,
we got a context size property on the language model. You see here, model.context size, and
it'll print out 496. But it's not just on the whole model. Oh, real quick, it is back deployed
before 26.4, so you can use it on previous versions of uh the OS, but you can do this per
element in what you're sending up to the model. So, your instructions, if you don't know, you give
your model instructions. Your helpful assistant that generates haikus. In my portfolio course,
I say you are a motivational fitness instructor. You know, I basically give it like two sentences
of instructions telling it what the model is so it knows how to act. But even the instructions
that uses tokens, see, in that case, it uses 16. If you're using tool calling, maybe you're digging
into the user's calendar to pull out their most recent events, or maybe you're making
a network call out to a weather API to get the recent weather to feed into your response. Well,
that tool call generates tokens, and you can see use the model token usage for instructions
and tools. And then it'll print out the token usage for that. So, the tool calling was 79. So,
you see these tokens start adding up. And then your prompt, you can see how much tokens you're
using for the prompt. In this case, it's 14. But it's just a reminder that uh don't just
ramble. I know when you're typing into the chatbt or claw, you're probably just typing away
having no consideration for how big your prompt actually is. So again, when you can actually print
out token count while you're building your app using foundation models, just knowing this is very
key because you want to keep this as minimal as possible while still being effective. And before
there wasn't really an easy way to see your token usage, but now in iOS 26.4, for we can really
break down each element of what we're sending up to the model to break down the token usage to make
sure we're being efficient and intelligent with how we're using that because again that context
window is tiny. I'm hoping they increase that over time. I assume they will but we'll see.
And finally, we have the Indie Apps Dev newsletter and Antoine Vanderly did a guest post on here
about AI adoption as an indie developer. And there's one thing I really want to focus on with
the context switching here, but I'll give you a quick overview about what he talks about. you know
reinventing ourselves kind of like I mentioned before like our entire job is changing like
in the past year what we do is completely changed dayto-day so he talks about that again this is the
context switching the building multiple apps at the same time I want to come back to that I want
to give you a quick overview first he gives guides to becoming better at aentic coding he gives his
workflow talks about always using plan mode which I agree because you know I just don't send out a
prompt and hope it oneshots it especially if it's a larger one I like to do plan mode because what
at least codeex that's what I use usually when I have a come up with a plan and I always use
extra high I have the max plan whatever it'll come back with you know four or five questions
about that plan clarifying how it should implement it or clarifying how it should handle an edge case
and a lot of times either I didn't even think of the edge case but even if I did think of the
edge case it recommends like potential solutions I didn't think of one of the potential solutions
and I'm like oh yeah that's a really good solution so I highly recommend plan mode as
well I know some people don't do that you know he says write test update your agents MD file
use skills like we talked about so he goes through his workflow but again the whole reason I wanted
to share this because I wanted to talk to you about context switching because this is what I
have really struggled with in this whole era of agentic coding because it is extremely enticing,
extremely tempting to just be like, "Oh my gosh, I can work on four or five projects at a time.
Let me send this agent out to app number one. All right, cool. While you're doing that, this
agent goes to app number two. Cool. While you're doing that, you go to app number three, etc."
It's very tempting to think that you can just do so much work now with all these agents. But
what I found is that that context switching between projects and what you're working on
is very difficult and like fries my brain to be honest. And what happens is like
I'll send it out on project one. Cool. While it's doing its thing, send it out on project two.
Send it out on project three. And then by the time one and two come back now I'm dealing with one
and then now I'm dealing with two's response and then by the time I actually get to project three,
I like forgot where I was and it's like confusing. Now of course this matters what you're doing. If
you're doing something super simple and mundane, boilerplate, like not a big deal. But trying to do
like big things on multiple projects I found doesn't work for me. And again, the reason
I wanted to share this was I want to hear how you're handling this context switching because
what I've done is I've just started focusing on one project at a time. And if I do need to work
on two projects in a day, I'll be like, "All right, in the morning I'm working on project
one. In the afternoon, I'm working on project two." Because I've just found context switching
on larger features that I have to like think about, especially when you're considering UI,
anything that requires creativity, right? Right. The UI requires creativity. The UX
requires creativity. Like making sure the actual flow and the user experience is good. So yeah,
how how are you handling the context switching if you're working on three or four projects at a
time? Do you have any strategies? I would love to hear it. I'm sure others would love to hear it in
the comments. But that's the that's the biggest thing I've been struggling with in this whole
era of agentic coding. That wraps it up for this episode of Swift News. See you in the next one.

# The State of Agentic iOS Engineering in 2026

## My perspective on AI-driven programming, workflows, and tooling

[![Thomas Ricouard](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*1qE8EhcYIUferlNrUnJjzQ.jpeg)](https://dimillian.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--c5f0cbaa7b34---------------------------------------)

[Thomas Ricouard](https://dimillian.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--c5f0cbaa7b34---------------------------------------)

Follow

22 min read

·

Jan 1, 2026

1K

11

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dc5f0cbaa7b34&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fdimillian.medium.com%2Fthe-state-of-agentic-ios-engineering-in-2026-c5f0cbaa7b34&source=---header_actions--c5f0cbaa7b34---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*6U7A_0DCB_YReRif1kV0Xw.png)

2025 was an AI-packed year, at least in my case. I don’t recall precisely how many times I changed my workflow, but I did experiment extensively. All in all, I can say that I’ve never worked so much and yet so little at the same time. You’ll understand what it means a bit later on.

Keeping up with AI trends is a full-time job, and I’m happy doing it. I’ve been motivated every day to improve my workflow, learn about new tooling, and overall improve my efficiency. It’s improving so fast, and as Andrej Karpathy [said](https://x.com/karpathy/status/2004621825180139522?s=20), “People who aren’t keeping up even over the last 30 days already have a deprecated worldview on this topic.” I agree with this statement. I’m not saying you have to, but I love being busy and learning, so keeping up with all this is just one of the things that bring me joy.

This article serves as a comprehensive 2025 debrief; the order of the sections doesn’t matter much, and I hope you’ll find a wealth of information about my workflow, agentic iOS programming, opinions, and much more, extending beyond our iOS world.

## The Deprecation of Xcode as a Text Editor

As iOS developers, we’re used to Xcode, but let me get this straight: I probably spent only 2% of my time actually in Xcode last year. The only savior was the [GitHub Copilot extension](https://github.com/github/CopilotForXcode). It allows you to get a Cursor-like experience, with fast and relevant AI completion on tab press within Xcode.

_When I say I’m not using Xcode extensively, I mean the Xcode text editor; iOS agentic workflow depends on many Xcode components accessed outside of Xcode, such as Swift LSP for Cursor Swift code highlighting and navigation to work, Xcodebuild for building, etc…_

![Screenshot 2025-12-31 at 12.46.21 PM.png](https://miro.medium.com/v2/fe14d4083cb16eae65cc5661718d260eaf9870f67855a2deab40517193741750)

However, recent updates have cluttered it with weird and broken UI choices, and you must disable most of the features to achieve a satisfactory experience.

However, I invite you to try it; it’s probably the most comfortable setup if you want or need to stay…

[![Thomas Ricouard](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:48:48/1*1qE8EhcYIUferlNrUnJjzQ.jpeg)](https://dimillian.medium.com/?source=post_page---post_author_info--c5f0cbaa7b34---------------------------------------)

[![Thomas Ricouard](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*1qE8EhcYIUferlNrUnJjzQ.jpeg)](https://dimillian.medium.com/?source=post_page---post_author_info--c5f0cbaa7b34---------------------------------------)
